title: Module 1: Introduction & Market
content: Module 1: Introduction & Market Overview

This module provides a clear, practical introduction to natural gas trading. It explains the importance of natural gas in the global energy market, outlines the main factors that drive its price, and describes the structure and goals of this course. This module also details a profitable trading strategy for natural gas futures and ETFs. The material is intended to build a solid foundation for the technical methods and strategies that will be covered in later modules.
1. Introduction to Natural Gas Trading

Natural gas is a widely used energy resource, supplying roughly 24% of the energy requirements of developed countries. It is essential for heating, electricity generation, and industrial processes. As a commodity, it has distinct characteristics and market behaviors that set it apart from other forms of energy. Trading natural gas involves understanding its production, distribution, and consumption patterns, as well as the various factors that cause price fluctuations.

title: 4. The Role of Natural Gas in the Global Energy Market
content: 4. The Role of Natural Gas in the Global Energy Market
Natural gas plays a critical role in today’s energy supply. It is used in a wide range of applications:

• Residential and Commercial Heating: Natural gas is a primary source for heating buildings. Around 29% of the world’s natural gas consumption is in heating buildings and other residential applications.
• Electricity Generation: Many power plants rely on natural gas for electricity production. Roughly 38% of the world’s natural gas consumption is for generating electricity.
• Industrial Use: It is a key raw material in chemical manufacturing and other industries. The industrial sector accounts for about 33% of global natural gas consumption.
Because natural gas is both abundant and relatively clean compared to other fossil fuels, it has become an important commodity worldwide. (There are leakage issues in transport.)
Understanding its role helps traders recognize why price movements can be significant and why the market is closely monitored by governments, industry players, and investors.

title: 5. Key Drivers of Natural Gas Prices
content: 5. Key Drivers of Natural Gas Prices
Successful trading depends on understanding the factors that influence natural gas prices. The main drivers include:
A. Supply and Demand
Supply Factors
• Production Levels: The amount of natural gas extracted from reserves.
• Storage Inventories: The levels of stored natural gas can signal whether the market is oversupplied or undersupplied.
• Extraction Technology: Advances in technology can increase production and influence price trends.
Demand Factors
• Consumption Patterns: Industrial, commercial, and residential usage all affect demand.
• Economic Activity: Growth or contraction in the economy influences energy consumption.
• Weather Conditions: Seasonal changes, such as winter cold or summer heat, have a strong impact on demand.
B. Seasonality
Natural gas prices often follow seasonal patterns:
• Winter: Demand typically rises for heating, leading to higher prices.

C. Geopolitical Influences
Political events and policies can have an immediate impact on natural gas markets:
• Trade Policies: Changes in international trade agreements can affect supply routes.
• Political Instability: Events in key producing regions may disrupt supply.
• Government Regulations: Policies regarding environmental standards and energy production can alter market dynamics.
D. Technological and Environmental Factors
• Technological Advances: New extraction methods, such as hydraulic fracturing, can increase supply and affect price trends.
• Environmental Policies: Regulations aimed at reducing emissions or promoting cleaner energy sources can influence both production and consumption.
All these factors work together to create the overall market environment. Traders must consider these drivers when analyzing market data and making trading decisions.

title: 5. How Market Data Informs Trading
content: 5. How Market Data Informs Trading
Accurate market data is essential for making informed trading decisions. In the natural gas market, several types of data are critical:
• Production Data: Information on the volume of natural gas being produced helps assess supply levels.
• Storage Reports: Data on stored natural gas provides insight into market balance.
• Consumption Figures: These reports show how much natural gas is being used in different sectors.
• Weather Forecasts: Since weather affects demand, accurate forecasts can predict price movements.
• Economic Indicators: Broader economic data can signal changes in energy consumption trends.

title: 7. Practical Considerations for New Traders
content: 7. Practical Considerations for New Traders
If you are new to natural gas trading, consider these practical tips:
• Build a Strong Foundation: Spend time understanding the basics of the natural gas market and the factors that drive price movements.
• Stay Informed: Regularly review market reports, weather forecasts, and economic indicators. Reliable data is key to making informed decisions.
• Practice Technical Analysis: Begin with simple candlestick chart analysis, and gradually learn to apply indicators and recognize patterns.
• Manage Risk Carefully: Always consider how much capital you are willing to risk on any trade and use tools such as stop-loss or hedge orders to protect your investment.
• Keep a Trading Journal: Document your trades, strategies, and observations. Reviewing your journal can help improve your decision-making over time.
These guidelines will help you build confidence as you start trading and prepare you for more advanced topics in later modules.

title: 1. Natural Gas 101
content: 1. Natural Gas 101
Natural gas is a fossil fuel composed primarily of methane, along with smaller amounts of other hydrocarbons. It is used in a variety of applications, including:

• Residential and Commercial Heating: Natural gas is a major source of heat in homes and businesses. Around 29% of the world’s natural gas consumption is for heating buildings and other residential applications.
• Electricity Generation: Power plants often use natural gas to generate electricity. Roughly 38% of the world’s natural gas consumption is for generating electricity.
• Industrial Processes: Many industries rely on natural gas as a feedstock or energy source. The industrial sector accounts for about 33% of global natural gas consumption.
Natural gas is valued for its relatively clean combustion compared to other fossil fuels, which has contributed to its growing use around the world.
2. Extraction and Production
Understanding how natural gas is extracted is key to grasping its market dynamics. The main methods of production include:
A. Conventional Extraction
• Drilling: Natural gas is extracted from reservoirs using traditional drilling methods.

title: • Well-Completion: Once a well is drilled, te...
content: • Well-Completion: Once a well is drilled, techniques such as perforating and fracturing may be used to allow gas to flow into the wellbore.
B. Unconventional Extraction

• Hydraulic Fracturing (Fracking): A process in which water, sand, and chemicals are injected at high pressure to create fractures in rock formations. This method has significantly increased the supply of natural gas.
• Horizontal Drilling: Often used in combination with fracking, horizontal drilling allows access to larger areas of gas-bearing rock.
C. Production Metrics
• Barrels of Oil Equivalent (BOE): A common measurement that expresses gas volumes in terms of energy content equivalent to oil.
• Cubic Feet and Million Cubic Feet (MMcf): Standard units for measuring the volume of natural gas.
Understanding production methods and metrics is important because changes in production levels can have a direct impact on natural gas prices.

title: Module 3: Trading Instruments –
content: Module 3: Trading Instruments – Futures and Continuous ETFs

This module provides a clear overview of the two primary instruments I use in natural gas trading: futures contracts and continuous exchange-traded funds (ETFs). Each instrument has its own features, benefits, and risks. In this module, we define both instruments, explain how they work, discuss their characteristics, and help you decide which instrument fits your trading style and market situation.
1. Overview of Trading Instruments
Natural gas retail traders (you and me!) have two main ways to participate in the market:
• Futures Contracts: Standardized agreements to buy or sell natural gas at a future date and at a predetermined price.
• Continuous Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs): Investment funds that track natural gas price movements and trade like stocks on an exchange.

Understanding these instruments is important because each offers different opportunities in terms of leverage, liquidity, and risk management. In the sections below, we discuss each instrument in detail.

title: Natural Gas Futures
content: Natural Gas Futures
Definition and Characteristics

A natural gas futures contract is a legally binding agreement to purchase or sell a specific quantity of natural gas at a predetermined price on a specified future date. These contracts are standardized, meaning that the terms of the contract, such as the quantity and quality of natural gas, are set by the exchange on which they trade (for example, the New York Mercantile Exchange, or NYMEX).
👉 Tickers: NGAS.F (BlackBull Markets) or NG1 (NYMEX futures)
Contract Specifications
Key features of natural gas futures include:
• Contract Size: Futures contracts specify a fixed amount of natural gas (often measured in million British thermal units or MMBtu).
• Expiration Date: Each contract has a set delivery month and expiration date.
• Tick Size: This is the minimum price movement for a contract. Tick size and value are important for determining potential profits or losses.
• Settlement: Futures contracts can be settled physically (delivery of the commodity) or financially (cash settlement), depending on the contract specifications.

title: Leverage and Margin
content: Leverage and Margin

Futures trading involves the use of leverage, which means you can control a large position with a relatively small amount of capital. Traders must post margin - a percentage of the contract’s value - as collateral. Leverage amplifies both gains and losses, making risk management critical.
Liquidity and Volatility
• Liquidity: Natural gas futures are traded on regulated exchanges, which typically offer high liquidity. High liquidity ensures that orders can be filled quickly with minimal price impact.
• Volatility: Prices in the futures market can be volatile. Volatility can create trading opportunities but also increases risk.
Advantages and Risks
Advantages:
● “Purity” - the futures provide the most accurate
representation of the market for traders.
● No daily carry costs. ● Standardized contracts and regulated trading environments. ● Ability to use leverage. ● High liquidity in major contracts.
Risks:
● Monthly roll, contango and backwardation. ● Exposure to rapid price changes due to market volatility.

title: Continuous Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs)
content: Continuous Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs)
Definition and Characteristics

Natural gas ETFs are investment funds that aim to track the performance of natural gas prices. ETFs trade on stock exchanges and offer a way to gain exposure to natural gas without directly engaging in futures or spot transactions. They typically invest in a portfolio of natural gas-related assets or in futures contracts that replicate the price movements of the commodity.
👉 Ticker: NATGAS (BlackBull continuous)
👉 Note on BOIL and KOLD

⚠ BOIL and KOLD are 2x leveraged ETFs that track short-term natural gas futures. They’re designed for daily trading, not holding - due to decay, volatility drag, and compounding errors, especially in choppy markets. Retail traders often get wrecked by holding too long, mistiming entries, or misunderstanding how these ETFs diverge from actual NATGAS prices. In short: they move fast, bleed value over time, and can burn both ends of your account. Best left to the pros - or masochists.
How ETFs Track Natural Gas Prices
ETFs can track natural gas prices through different methods:
Physical Replication: Some ETFs hold physical assets or contracts that mirror natural gas prices.
Synthetic Replication: Other ETFs use derivatives, such as futures contracts, to replicate price movements.

title: Practical Considerations and Examples
content: Practical Considerations and Examples
When choosing an instrument for natural gas trading, consider your trading style, risk tolerance, and time horizon. Below are practical scenarios for each instrument:
Futures Trading Example

A trader who monitors market fundamentals and technical indicators might use futures to take advantage of an anticipated price move. For instance, if forecasts and inventory data suggest a supply disruption, the trader may buy a natural gas futures contract. With high leverage, even a small price increase can result in a significant profit. However, the trader must set stop-loss orders to manage the risk of a volatile market.
ETF Trading Example

An investor seeking exposure to natural gas with lower risk might opt for an ETF. Suppose an investor wants to benefit from gradual price increases over a longer period without the complexities of margin trading. In that case, an ETF provides a simple and accessible way to participate. The investor should monitor the ETF’s tracking performance relative to natural gas prices and consider management fees in their overall strategy.

title: Contango vs. Backwardation:
content: Contango vs. Backwardation:

In a contango market, rolling contracts can incur a cost (negative roll yield). Conversely, in backwardation, roll yields can be positive. Monitor the futures curve and adjust your strategy accordingly.
Alternative Instruments:
When roll costs are significant, consider trading continuous ETFs to mitigate the impact of contract rollovers.

Identify overbought and oversold conditions. Divergence between RSI and price movement can signal that a trend is due for a reversal.

Monitor the width of Bollinger Bands for volatility signals. A “third pierce” of the band can indicate an extreme condition, suggesting a reversal is likely.

Retracement Levels:
Apply Fibonacci retracement levels (38.2%, 50%, 61.8%) to identify potential pullback areas during a trending move. These levels often coincide with key support or resistance zones.
Extensions for Targets:
Use Fibonacci extension levels to set profit targets once a trend resumes. This method can help in planning exit points and scaling out of po]sitions.

title: Hedging with Options and Futures
content: Hedging with Options and Futures
• Protective Options:
Incorporate put options to hedge against adverse price movements while maintaining a] long position. Similarly, call options can hedge short positions.
• Futures-Based Hedges:
Use opposing futures positions to offset exposure in your primary trade. For instance, if you have a long technical setup, consider a short hedge on an adjacent contract to limit downside risk.
Managing Roll Strategies:

Monitor the futures curve carefully. In contango, be prepared for negative roll yields; in backwardation, take advantage of the favorable roll. Adjust your strategy - possibly switching to continuous ETFs - if roll costs become significant.

content: Module 7: Risk Management & Position Sizing for Technical Traders

This module focuses on managing risk and determining appropriate position sizes - a critical part of successful natural gas trading. In volatile markets, effective risk management protects your capital and helps ensure long-term success.
👉 Watch this video (again!)
1. The Importance of Risk Management
Trading natural gas involves inherent risks due to market volatility, leverage, and unpredictable fundamental factors such as weather and geopolitical events. A robust risk management plan:
● Protects your trading capital. ● Helps prevent large losses on any single trade. ● Keeps your overall strategy on track over the long term. ● Keeps you sane!
2. Core Risk Management Principles
Setting Stop-Loss Orders
Definition:
A stop-loss order automatically exits a trade when the price moves against you by a predetermined amount. We prefer to hedge. See above.

title: Placement:
content: Placement:
Use technical levels - such as support/resistance zones, trend lines, or moving averages - to set stop-loss levels.
Example:
If you enter a long trade near a key support level, set your stop-loss just below that level to limit potential losses.
Determining Risk-to-Reward Ratio
Concept:
The risk-to-reward ratio compares the potential loss of a trade to the expected profit. A common goal is to aim for a minimum ratio of 1:2, meaning the potential reward is twice the potential risk.
Application:
Before entering a trade, define your exit levels (both stop-loss and profit target) to ensure that the potential profit justifies the risk taken.
3. Position Sizing
Calculating Position Size
Risk per Trade:
Determine the maximum percentage of your total capital you are willing to risk on a single trade (commonly 1–2%).

title: entry price and stop-loss price)
content: Position size = (Amount at risk per trade) / (Difference between entry price and stop-loss price)
Example Calculation:

If you have a $10,000 trading account and decide to risk 1% ($100) per trade, and the difference between your entry and stop-loss is $0.50 per unit, your position size would be 200 units (i.e., $100 / $0.50).
Adjusting for Leverage
When trading futures, leverage can amplify gains and losses. Always ensure your position size and risk calculations account for the leverage used, so you do not expose yourself to excessive risk.
Managing Roll Risks
Contract Rollovers:
For front-month futures, monitor the roll period carefully. The cost or benefit from rolling (contango or backwardation) can affect your overall risk.
Mitigation:
Adjust your strategy by planning your roll timing or by using alternative instruments (such as continuous ETFs) if roll costs are significant.

title: • Objective Analysis: A disciplined approach ensures that
content: • Objective Analysis: A disciplined approach ensures that decisions are based on data and predefined criteria rather than emotions.
• Consistency: Maintaining a consistent mindset and strategy helps prevent costly mistakes over time.
Common Psychological Challenges
Traders often face similar mental obstacles. Understanding these challenges is essential for developing effective coping strategies.
Fear and Anxiety
• Fear of Loss: The potential for loss may cause hesitation or premature exit from a position.
• Uncertainty: Unpredictable market movements can lead to anxiety, impacting judgment.
Greed and Overconfidence
• Greed: The desire for larger profits can lead to overtrading or ignoring risk management rules.
• Overconfidence: A series of winning trades may result in excessive risk-taking or deviation from the trading plan.
Impulse Trading and Cognitive Biases
• Impulse Trading: Decisions made on impulse rather than through analysis can lead to erratic results.
• Cognitive Biases: Confirmation bias, recency bias, and other mental shortcuts can distort one’s view of the market.

title: 3. Strategies for Developing Discipline
content: 3. Strategies for Developing Discipline
Discipline is achieved through preparation, planning, and consistent practice. The following strategies help maintain a disciplined approach:
Creating and Following a Trading Plan
• Define Clear Rules: Outline entry and exit criteria, risk management parameters, and position sizing.
• Stick to the Plan: Avoid making ad-hoc decisions during periods of market volatility.
• Review and Update: Regularly review your trading plan and adjust it based on performance and new insights.
Maintaining a Trading Journal
• Record Keeping: Document each trade with details on the setup, the rationale for the trade, entry and exit points, and the outcome.
• Reflect on Mistakes: Analyze unsuccessful trades to identify patterns and learn from errors.
• Track Emotions: Note how you felt before, during, and after trades. Understanding your emotional state can reveal triggers that need management.
Building a Routine
• Consistent Schedule: Establish a daily routine that includes market analysis, trade planning, and post-market review.

title: 1. Introduction
content: 1. Introduction

The Commitments of Traders (COT) report is an essential tool for understanding market sentiment. Barchart.com presents COT data in a user-friendly format, allowing traders to review the positions held by different market participants. By analyzing the net positions of commercials, managed money, and non-commercial traders, you can gain valuable clues about future price movements. This module will guide you through using this data effectively.
2. Understanding the COT Report
Report Structure and Categories
Barchart.com displays the COT report with clear categories that help you distinguish between various types of market participants:
• Commercials: Typically producers and hedgers who use the market to manage price risk.
• Managed Money: Represents large speculative traders such as hedge funds and commodity trading advisors.
• Non-Commercials: Often includes other large speculators who trade for profit.
• Other Reportables: Smaller traders who do not fit into the main categories.
Each category shows both long and short positions. The key metric to monitor is the net position (the difference between long and short positions) for each group.
Key Data Points

title: indication of overall market sentiment among that group.
content: • Net Positions: A net long or net short reading provides an indication of overall market sentiment among that group.
• Changes Over Time: Monitor week-to-week changes to spot emerging trends. Sharp shifts can signal a change in market direction.
• Comparison to Historical Norms: Identifying when positions are at extreme levels relative to historical averages can help flag potential turning points.
3. Analyzing Positioning Data
Interpreting Net Positions
• Commercials:

A net short position among commercials typically indicates that hedgers expect lower prices, as they aim to protect against a decline. Conversely, a net long position might suggest producers are locking in higher prices.
• Managed Money and Non-Commercials:
These groups are generally speculative. Extreme net long or net short positions may signal that a market reversal is due, as speculative excess often precedes a correction.
Recognizing Divergence
Compare the COT data with current price trends:
• If the price continues in one direction while net positions begin to reverse, it may indicate that the prevailing trend is becoming overextended.

• Weekly Reviews:

Module 11: Appendices and Additional Resources

This final module is designed to serve as a comprehensive reference and resource guide for your continued development as a natural gas trader. In this module, you will find a glossary of key terms, a list of recommended reading and resources, information on useful tools and platforms, and tips for ongoing education and networking.
1. Glossary of Key Terms
Understanding the specialized language of natural gas trading is essential. Below are some frequently used terms:
• Contango: A market condition where futures prices are higher than the current spot price.
• Backwardation: The opposite of contango; futures prices are lower than the spot price.
• Candlestick Pattern: A method of charting price action that displays open, high, low, and close values.
• RSI (Relative Strength Index): An oscillator used to measure the speed and change of price movements.

• Contango: A market condition where futures prices are higher 
than the current spot price. 

• Backwardation: The opposite of contango; futures prices are 
lower than the spot price.

displays open, high, low, and close values. 

• RSI (Relative Strength Index): An oscillator used to measure

title: • MACD (Moving Average Convergence Divergence): An
content: • MACD (Moving Average Convergence Divergence): An indicator that shows the relationship between two moving averages of a security’s price.
• EIA: The Energy Information Administration, which provides key data on natural gas storage and production.
• COT Report: The Commitments of Traders report, detailing market positioning of various trader groups.
• Futures Contract: A standardized legal agreement to buy or sell a specific quantity of natural gas at a predetermined price at a future date.
• Spread Trading: A strategy that involves taking opposing positions in related contracts to profit from the price differential.

content: During Market Hours:
• Monitor Market Conditions:
• Continuously review real-time charts for price action and volume spikes.
• Stay updated with live news and any unexpected market developments.
• Trade Setup Confirmation:
• Look for technical signals (such as reversal patterns, breakout formations) confirmed by fundamental data.
• Check that risk management parameters (stop-loss, target levels, position sizing) are in place.

• Enter trades according to your predefined plan.
• Set or adjust stop-loss and take-profit orders as needed.

title: Trade Management:
content: Trade Management:
• Active Monitoring:
• Regularly review your open positions, using multi-timeframe analysis to validate trade performance.
• Adjust stops and hedges based on evolving technical and fundamental signals.
• Documentation:
• Log every trade in your trading journal—record entry/exit points, reasons for the trade, and any adjustments made.
• Capture screenshots of your charts for future reference.
Trade Exit:
• Execute Exit Strategy:
• Exit trades when profit targets are hit or stop-loss levels are triggered.
• If market conditions change unexpectedly, reassess and close positions as necessary.
